sections.content: Six consonant rules separate Ecclesiastical Latin from Classical Latin. These are the sounds that make Church Latin sound like Church Latin. Each rule is simple on its own — together they transform how Latin sounds.

sections.title: Rule 1: Soft C
sections.content:
C before E, I, AE, or OE is soft — pronounced 'ch' as in 'church.'
C before A, O, U, or a consonant remains hard — 'k' as in 'cat.'

Soft C examples:
• cælum (heaven) = CHAY-loom
• Ecce (behold) = ET-chay
• dulcédo (sweetness) = dool-CHAY-doh
• pace (peace) = PAH-chay

Hard C examples:
• corpus (body) = KOR-poos
• cum (with) = koom
• Credo (I believe) = KRAY-doh

sections.title: Rule 2: Soft G
sections.content:
G before E or I is soft — pronounced 'j' as in 'gentle.'
G before A, O, U, or a consonant remains hard — 'g' as in 'go.'

Soft G examples:
• Regína (Queen) = ray-JEE-nah
• Génetrix (Mother) = JAY-nay-triks
• vigília (vigil) = vee-JEE-lee-ah

Hard G examples:
• glória (glory) = GLOH-ree-ah
• grátia (grace) = GRAH-tsee-ah
• Dóminus (Lord) = DOH-mee-noos (no G, but note: hard G rule applies when G is present)

sections.title: Rule 3: V is Always V
sections.content:
In Classical Latin, V was pronounced 'w.' In Ecclesiastical Latin, V is always 'v' as in 'vine.'

• vita (life) = VEE-tah, not 'WEE-tah'
• Ave (hail) = AH-vay, not 'AH-way'
• vobíscum (with you) = voh-BEE-skoom
• Virgo (Virgin) = VEER-goh

sections.title: Rule 4: TI Before a Vowel
sections.content:
TI before a vowel is pronounced 'tsee' (like the 'zz' in 'pizza').
Exception: TI after S, X, or T keeps the regular 'tee' sound.

• grátia (grace) = GRAH-tsee-ah
• natióne (nation) = nah-tsee-OH-nay
• oratiónem (prayer) = oh-rah-tsee-OH-nem

Exceptions:
• Hóstia (host) = OH-stee-ah (after S, so regular TI)
• bestia (beast) = BEH-stee-ah

sections.title: Rule 5: GN
sections.content:
GN is pronounced 'ny' — like the 'ñ' in Spanish 'canyon' or 'lasagna.'

• Agnus (Lamb) = AHN-yoos
• Regnum (kingdom) = RAIN-yoom
• Signum (sign) = SEEN-yoom
• Magnus (great) = MAHN-yoos
• dignus (worthy) = DEEN-yoos

sections.title: Rule 6: Double Consonants
sections.content:
Double consonants are both pronounced — distinctly and separately. This is unlike English where doubles are usually silent.

• Ecce (behold) = ET-chay (two C sounds)
• bellum (war) = BEL-loom (hold the L)
• anno (year) = AHN-noh (hold the N)
• missa (Mass) = MEES-sah (hold the S)
• terra (earth) = TEHR-rah (roll the R)

items.rules: Double C, soft S before I

sections.content: Six rules: (1) C before e/i = 'ch,' (2) G before e/i = 'j,' (3) V = 'v' always, (4) TI before vowel = 'tsee,' (5) GN = 'ny,' (6) double consonants both pronounced. With vowels from Course I, you can now read any Latin prayer aloud.
